data.title: LendingClub: Irrational Earnings Sell Off
data.description:
LendingClub (LC) headed into the Q4'25 earnings report trading at multi-year highs above $20, so the pre- and post-earnings dip needs to be taken into perspective. The fintech remains relatively conservative with growth prospects, preferring to throw off a lot of cash vs. ramping up spending. My investment thesis remains ultra bullish on the stock, especially after the post-earnings dip.

Strong Q4

LendingClub reported a strong Q4 2025 with the following quarterly numbers as follows:

The company reported Q4 loan originations grew by 40% to reach $2.6 billion. The market was likely disappointed the loan originations dipped sequentially, but management has long cautioned that the December quarter was always weaker due to the holidays.

LendingClub grew the yearly originations to $10 billion and guided to 2026 goals of $11.6 billion to $12.6 billion. At the midpoint, the fintech is only forecasting quarterly loan originations in the $3 billion range, likely disappointing investors hoping for a faster return to and above peak levels of nearly $4 billion.

Back in Q2'22, LendingClub generated $330 million in quarterly revenue with loan originations soaring to $3.8 billion. The economy has grown significantly since 2022, and credit levels are much higher now, but the fintech isn't forecasting for the 2026 quarterly loan originations to match those peak levels.

The stock has slumped to $17, while LendingClub guided to a 2026 EPS of $1.65 to $1.80. Naturally, the stock gets very cheap at only 10x the low-end EPS target.

Fair Value Shift

The 2026 numbers are slightly convoluted due to the shift to valuing loans held for investment to fair value accounting. LendingClub will remove the CECL provision cost front loaded into the cost structure and now adjust loan values on a quarterly basis.

The Fair Value Option will apply to all newly originated loans starting on Jan. 1. Remember, LendingClub already took the upfront CECL charges on existing loans. The company provides this handy chart of how the Fair Value Option pulls earnings forward, though in theory the loans produce the same earnings over the loan period of up to five years.

Investors are probably looking at the EPS guidance and calculating LendingClub should likely produce higher EPS in 2026. The bank just earned a combined $0.72 over 2H'25 for an annualized EPS rate of $1.44 under the prior valuation method with the upfront CECL cost.

Remember though, LendingClub is historically very conservative. The company originally guided to only a loan origination goal of greater than $2.3 billion for Q4'25, while the company actually reached nearly $2.6 billion.

Also, management is guiding toward increasing marketing expenses this year to offset some of the benefits. In addition, the company forecast existing loans on the balance sheet are forecast to take an additional $10 million charge, making these initial 2026 earnings reports messy.

Regardless, LendingClub guided to a 2026 EPS of $1.73 at the midpoint, up around 50% YoY from the 2025 numbers. Most of this growth comes from the leverage inherent in a forecast for loan origination growth of 26% in 2026.

The stock has a tangible book value per share at $12.30 now. LendingClub has nearly $90 million left on the stock repurchase program, and this dip below $17 provides an opportune time to acquire shares.

Naturally, the biggest risk to the investment story is a quick change in the economy. The big question is what happens to bank and insurance company loan investors on the next weak credit cycle. The stock traded below TBV down below $9 last April when the stock market was in panic mode.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that LendingClub is insanely cheap, trading at only 10x EPS targets while loan originations are growing at a mid-20% growth rate. The fintech should trade at a much higher multiple to correspond with growth.

Investors should use this weakness to load up on LendingClub, though most investors should already have a full position in the stock, buying at much lower levels.

data.title: HEDJ: European Large-Cap Equity Exposure With A Currency Hedge
data.description:
The WisdomTree Europe Hedged Equity Fund ETF (HEDJ) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to the European equity market paired with a currency hedging strategy between the US Dollar and the Euro. The strategy can be best utilized by investors seeking to hedge risk to US equities by investing in European equities while maintaining neutral currency exposure. HEDJ has a scheduled quarterly distribution, but has varied from period to period with some quarters not paying out a distribution, making the strategy most appropriate for capital appreciation rather than income.

About WisdomTree Europe Hedged Equity Fund ETF

HEDJ was launched on December 31, 2009 by WisdomTree on the NYSE Arca Exchange and has a net expense ratio of 58bps. HEDJ has roughly $1.82 billion in net assets with an average of $2.24 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis, presenting modest bid/ask spread risk with an average rate of 0.28%.

HEDJ pays out quarterly distributions that may vary from period to period with some quarters not paying out. Distributions are based in income generated from dividend payments by portfolio holdings and are taxed as ordinary income. Over the last twelve months, HEDJ has paid out $0.87/share, yielding 1.60%. Given the variability in the distribution rate and the relatively low yield, HEDJ may not be appropriate for income-oriented investors.

HEDJ was designed to track the performance of the WisdomTree European Hedged Equity Index. The Index was constructed to include European-domiciled, dividend-paying companies within the WisdomTree International Equity Index that are traded in Euros. Constituents must have a minimum market capitalization of $1 billion and derive at least 50% of revenue from countries outside of Europe. Constituents are weighted based on annual cash dividends. Individual constituents are capped at a 5% weight; sectors are capped at 25%; countries are capped at 25%.

The Index is heavily weighted in large-cap equities, making up 86% of the total portfolio weight. By region, the Index is most heavily weighted in Germany at 23.70%, France at 23.34%, Spain at 18%, and the Netherlands at 16.54%. By industry, the Index is most heavily weighted in capital goods, making up 21.49% of net assets.

As of January 27, 2026, the aggregate price/earnings ratio of the total portfolio was 16.49x. For reference, the S&P 500 Value Index (IVE) has a P/E ratio of 24.09x.

HEDJ is diversified across 129 individual holdings with the top 10 holdings making up 42% of net assets. In contrast, the bottom 10 holdings amount to 0.01% of the total portfolio weight, making the portfolio relatively concentrated to the top components. The top holdings within the ETF include Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria S.A. (BBVA) at 6.53%, ASML Holding NV (ASML) at 5.67%, and LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton SE (LVMHF) at 5.09%.

Why Invest in European Equities

Investing in international equities may provide investors with differentiated returns while acting as a hedge against domestic equities. A defining factor is the relatively lower trading premium of European equities relative to large-cap US stocks, which may provide investors with a greater value lean as part of their broader portfolio strategy. European equities may also be utilized to diversify and gain regional exposure. From a macro perspective, investors may generally gain differentiated exposure to regional monetary policy, inflation, and GDP growth.

HEDJ provides a unique contrast to the broader international investment theme with the Index selecting constituents based on their international trade exposure. From a high level, this could be a unique strategy that may lean into US/EU trade relations where a market for increasing European imports may facilitate growth in HEDJ. With HEDJ hedging currency risk, the ETF may provide returns that closely track the performance of the underlying holdings with minimal foreign exchange influence.

Investor Suitability

HEDJ may be best utilized by investors that expect European equities to outperform relative to the domestic market. Given the relatively low distribution yield and uneven quarterly distributions, HEDJ may not be appropriate for income-oriented investors. For asset allocation, HEDJ can be utilized for international exposure as part of a diversified portfolio strategy and may be paired with an international fixed income strategy.

Risks Related to HEDJ

HEDJ may expose investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. HEDJ may expose investors to regional macroeconomic risks like interest rate policy, inflation, manufacturing productivity, and regional GDP growth. HEDJ is a passively managed investment strategy, meaning that the portfolio may not offer country or sector rotation, potentially creating greater exposure to regional risk. European equities have historically traded at a discount relative to US equities and may not grow to match trading premiums.

Final Thoughts

HEDJ can be utilized to gain international equity exposure as part of a diversified portfolio strategy. Given the currency hedge feature in the strategy, HEDJ may provide investors with a more direct investment strategy in European equities with foreign exchange providing minimal influence to the value of the fund.

This article answers three main questions about HEDJ:

  1. What kind of companies does HEDJ hold?
  2. How does HEDJ currency hedge influence its role in a portfolio?
  3. Who is HEDJ most appropriate for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Royal Caribbean Cruises Has Gifted Investors A Great Trip
data.description:
January 29th was a fantastic day for shareholders of cruise ship operator Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL). Shares of the company rose around 18.7% after management announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Even though revenue and earnings per share both fell short of analysts’ expectations, the company did come out with guidance that points toward a positive 2026. This helps to paint the company in a favorable light. And it helps to explain why the stock is currently up 15.2% since I reaffirmed it as a Hold candidate back in October of last year.

Conceptually, the company is fascinating. It is healthier than other players in the space such as Carnival Corporation (CCL) and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH). But at the same time as this, it is also more expensive. I understand paying a premium for quality. But the stock really is getting quite lofty. I won't go so far as to say the down grading it to something more bearish is appropriate at this time. But I do believe that investors should be cautious moving forward, especially because the stock is very solidly in the fair value range as I define it.

A rough quarter, but a bright future

On January 29th, before the market opened, the management team at Royal Caribbean Cruises announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue for that time came in at $4.26 billion. That represented an increase of 13.2% over the $3.76 billion the company reported a year earlier. This overall growth was driven by a couple of factors. Occupancy at its cruise ships inched up from 107.6% to 107.8%. But even more impressive was a rise in the number of passengers carried from 2.16 million to 2.48 million. Passenger cruise days for the company grew from 13.68 million to 15.12 million. And another metric, known as APCD, or available passenger cruise days, grew from 12.72 million to 14.03 million. These increases are all encouraging, and they point to a very healthy company in a space that continues to expand.

Management succeeded in boosting occupancy year over year. It has 69 ships sailing to over 1,000 destinations across all seven continents. This compares to the 66 ships that the company had a year earlier. The overall rise in capacity that the company saw during 2025 in its entirety was 5.5%. In addition to growing the size of its fleet, it continues to benefit from an increase in the numbers and types of experiences that it offers. For 2026, we can expect this trend to continue. For instance, on the same day that earnings were announced, the company revealed multiple agreements with a shipyard in France to construct its new Discovery Class ships. The first of these will be berthed in 2029, with the second coming out in 2032. It also unveiled q commitment for 10 new ships to grow its river cruise fleet to 20 vessels by 2031.

If everything goes according to plan, then in 2026, overall capacity should rise by 6.7%. This should be followed up by a capacity increase of 4% in 2027, 6% in 2028, and 7% in 2029. And when it comes to specific offerings, the company said that it is growing its portfolio of private destinations from three to eight by 2028. And it is entering river cruising in 2027 with a new service called Celebrity River Cruises.

As revenue for the company increased, profitability improved also. Earnings per share jumped from $2.02 to $2.76. This ended up missing analysts’ expectations by $0.06, but it still meant a rise in net profits from $553 million to $754 million. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share of $2.80 beat out the $1.63 reported a year earlier.

That fell in line with what analysts were hoping to see. And it meant an increase in adjusted net profits from $445 million just $762 million. Other profitability metrics improved also. Operating cash flow expanded from $1.47 billion to $1.62 billion. On an adjusted basis that strips out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $1.05 billion to $1.19 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $1.10 billion to $1.48 billion.

The chart above shows financial results for 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024. Thanks to the same factors that allowed growth in the final quarter on a year over year basis, revenue, profits, and cash flows, or increased nicely. And because of the growth in capacity and the corresponding rise in APCDs from 53.33 million last year to an expected 56.9 million this year, the company expects revenue to grow enough to push adjusted earnings per share to between $17.70 and $18.10. At the midpoint, we would be looking at adjusted net profits of $4.89 billion. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of $6.94 billion and EBITDA of around $8.01 billion.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. I also compared the firm, in that chart, to two other similar firms. What we see here, unfortunately, is that Royal Caribbean Cruises is quite a bit more expensive than these other two players. This is disappointing to say the least. Sometimes, valuation disparities like this can be justified. But that's usually when there is some fundamental reason behind it.

One thing that I can point to that is special about Royal Caribbean Cruises is that its overall debt profile is attractive. With net debt of $20.52 billion, the net leverage ratio for the business comes in at only 2.92. As the chart below illustrates, this is measurably lower than with the other two competitors I stacked it up against have.

One last thing that I would like to touch on briefly is the expectation for the industry on a global scale. In a previous article about Royal Caribbean Cruises, I highlighted how the Cruise Lines International Association expected the number of passengers on cruise ships to continue climbing. This was from a 2025 report, and I could not locate a new report yet for 2026. So basing it off of those same assumptions, this year the industry should grow to 39.6 million passengers. That's up from the 37.7 million estimated for 2025. And by 2028, we are looking at total global passengers of about 41.9 million.

This means that the capacity additions that management is making are unlikely to have a big negative impact on profitability. If anything, the size and scale of the business should give it more power to extract profits compared to smaller and less competitive players.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I remain optimistic about Royal Caribbean Cruises. The company is growing nicely. It's a high quality player with lower risk than other similar firms. But this doesn't necessarily make it an ideal prospect to buy into. Shares are expensive, and it is my belief that there are better opportunities to be had because of that. At the end of the day, I think that this justifies more of a Hold candidate than it does anything else.

data.title: Fresenius Medical Care: Low Confidence On Growth Acceleration Limits Upside
data.description:
Despite well over 20 years of free cash flow growth, Fresenius Medical Care (FMS) shares have gone basically nowhere in the last 15 years on a net basis, and have only appreciated a little more than 3% a year since 2005. This is a case in point as to why low-growth med-tech is such a treacherous space, as institutional investors by and large just do not care about these stocks.

I’m not too bullish on the upcoming quarterly release due to a more severe U.S. flu season, and I have my doubts whether the company can grow core earnings much beyond 3%-4% over the next 10+ years, let alone achieve enough margin leverage in the near term to really excite the Street. While valuation looks undemanding on many levels, and this could be a name that returns meaningful capital to shareholders in the future, this looks like a tough place to generate meaningful alpha.

Mixed Trends Going Into Q4 Results

Fresenius reported some objectively strong results in Q3, with revenue up about 10% and operating income up 28% in constant currency, including double-digit growth in both Care Delivery and Care Enablement segment earnings.

I’m not so bullish on a repeat in the fourth quarter. Elevated mortality in the U.S. is still weighing on underlying dialysis volumes, and a severe flu season is likely to lead to even more people skipping some of their dialysis sessions, so there is some downside risk to the flat same-market growth that the company saw in Q3. I’m also concerned about headwinds from equipment sales in China, with value-based procurement the main issue there. All told, I think 4% organic growth may be the more likely number when they report as opposed to the 5% sell-side expectation.

I’m expecting another good-looking year-over-year comp in operating income growth (likely in the low-20%s), helped in part by a consent payment in the U.S. pharma operations and ongoing execution on the strategic cost efficiency program. The Value-Based Care business is likely to remain in the red, but more progress on getting to breakeven would be welcome.

Looking ahead to 2026, I’m concerned about whether Fresenius can drive meaningful progress in a more challenging environment. I don’t see evidence that the elevated mortality trend in the U.S. that has hampered volume growth is abating, and I expect some headwinds from ACA subsidy expiries and non-repeating benefits from phosphate binders. I also expect the company to incur some elevated costs as they begin rolling out high-volume hemodiafiltration (or HVHDF) systems in the United States. All told, I see a risk of some guide-down relative to where sell-side expectations have been, but I’d also note that sell-side expectations for 2026 have been falling in recent weeks as analysts preview Q4 earnings guidance for 2026.

Core Growth Dynamics Remain Muted

While limited competition in a market is usually good for margins, that’s not the case in dialysis – Fresenius and DaVita (DVA) make up about three-quarters of the U.S. hemodialysis market, but the reality is that the U.S. government funds most dialysis care in the country, and the government’s payment rates are such that most centers can only hope to breakeven. To that end, clinics typically make all of their profits from the 33-month period in which new dialysis patients are still covered by the commercial insurance plans.

I see no likelihood of the U.S. federal government increasing its payment rates for dialysis treatments. Likewise, I don’t see any real prospects for increased use of home dialysis or peritoneal dialysis; the former requires quite a bit of space and good health (and/or help), the latter is likewise more challenging for most patients than in-center dialysis, and there’s little economic incentive for Fresenius, et al., to push it harder.

While patient flows have been fairly stable, it remains to be seen if that will remain the case over the next five-plus years. Diabetes has long been a significant driver of end-stage renal disease, but newer medications like SGLT-2 and GLP-1 inhibitors are reno-protective and could reduce progression to end-stage renal disease – management has argued that long-term compliance is lower than commonly believed, and the cardio-protective benefits of these drugs may ultimately offset the near-term pressures on ESRD progression (if people are living longer, some of them will still develop ESRD).

More bundled services and new technology could be a key long-term driver for improved margins over time. Services include vascular care (including maintaining access patency) and pharmaceuticals, with dialysis patients often requiring treatments for anemia (erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or EPAs, IV iron, and/or HIF-PH inhibitors), phosphate management, hyperparathyroidism, and pruritus. With more of these treatments shifting toward IV admin, there are some ongoing fee-for-service opportunities here.

Fresenius’s Care Enablement business includes dialysis machines and dialyzers (where it has around 90% U.S. share), as well as consumables (roughly two-thirds share). Within this segment, the company developed high-volume hemodiafiltration (or HVHDF) and is now looking to make it a standard of care in the United States.

This technology removes a higher percentage of the toxins than standard dialysis, and the CONVINCE study showed both a lower risk of death (hazard ratio of 0.77) and better outcomes across multiple quality-of-life metrics. HVHDF appears to drive around 18 months of incremental survival, with improved quality of life along the way, and that should be a compelling argument for the technology. In addition to equipment sale opportunities, there’s an opportunity for Fresenius to capture an even higher percentage of dialysis consumables, with nearly 100% share conceivable in some cases.

Looking For Growth Outside Of The Clinic

Challenges to the core dialysis service operations are nothing new, and Fresenius has actively pursued other lines of business to drive incremental revenue and profits. There are some opportunities here, but meaningful challenges as well.

Fresenius’s Value-Based Care is a business where FMS is paid on the basis of patient outcomes and total care cost and is basically an insurance model (versus the more typical fee-for-service model). The company contracts with public and private payers and essentially uses its assets (nephrologists, analytics, care coordination, et al) to manage patients across education and care.

Fresenius already has a low double-digit share across chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease in the U.S., and the main idea here is to maximize patient outcomes and minimize more expensive health care utilization.

Nearly half of dialysis patients “crash into” dialysis with no preparation, and these patients often do not know how to manage their disease at home and have to initially rely on central venous catheters for dialysis instead of fistulae/grafts, all of which drives well above-average hospital visits and higher bills (often thousands, or tens of thousands of dollars for payors), as well as higher morbidity and mortality.

With VBC, though, Fresenius can often reach these patients earlier and guide them through the process, preparing and educating them for an eventual transition to dialysis and achieving better outcomes across the board.

The “but” here is that this is a business with razor-thin models in a best-case scenario, and Fresenius is still some distance away from that best case. Margin in the third quarter was -3.8%, and management is hoping to get this up to the low single digits by 2030.

The Outlook

While Fresenius has been posting some better results of late, and is targeting mid-teens operating margins by 2030, I’m still concerned that the company’s overall growth prospects are rather lackluster.

Longer lifespans are likely to offset some population effects (Gen X is smaller than the Boomer generation), but I don’t see much growth in ESRD patient populations, particularly if newer medications like SGLT-2’s and GLP-1’s do meaningfully reduce long-term ESRD progression. I likewise don’t see any momentum for higher overall reimbursement rates for dialysis. This is likely to limit growth in dialysis services and equipment, while the expansion of dialysis availability in countries like China and India offers some potential; ability to pay is a key issue that is likely to limit the benefits of these volumes.

Value-Based Care offers more growth potential on the revenue side, but it remains to be seen whether the company can drive meaningful margins from this business.

Likewise, achieving better margins overall remains a key “show me story” with Fresenius. The company’s Reignite program hopes to drive mid-teens operating margins by 2030 through improved operating efficiency, tech utilization, and “operational excellence,” but I’m skeptical that they’ll reach and hold these improvements. Moreover, I’m concerned that even if they do achieve these targets, there will be increased pressure from payors to basically claw back some of those profits.

All told, I think 3% long-term annualized revenue growth is a reasonable expectation. On the margin side, I think EBITDA around 20% in 2030 is possible, but I think the company will only just get to its “mid-teens” target operating margin (around 14%). All of this should support high-single-digit free cash flow margins and around 4% long-term FCF growth, not far off the 5% average of the last 20+ years.

Valuation – It’s Complicated…

This is a more complicated and convoluted valuation discussion than normal, but I think it’s important to try to offer a little depth here. On a discounted cash flow basis, Fresenius looks quite undervalued – I can easily argue that 4% annualized FCF growth should support an annualized return of over 10% a year.

The “but” is that institutions just don’t care. Low-growth med-tech struggles to find much institutional support; Fresenius could well generate (and return) large amounts of capital for shareholders over the next decade, but institutional managers aren’t typically paid to sit and collect dividends, and it can be difficult to keep their attention beyond a year or two even with better growth.

It may be more reasonable to think of valuation in terms of core profit growth and P/E – I expect core operating earnings growth of around 3% over the next decade, and I think share buybacks could drive per-share core earnings growth closer to 6%. Give the shares a 1.5x PEG, so a 9x forward P/E, and I get to around today’s price for a fair value.

The Bottom Line

If you have a case for Fresenius achieving better sustained revenue, earnings, and cash flow growth than I expect, these shares could have some appeal – the valuation based on DCF and traditional med-tech metrics like revenue growth and EBITDA margins is not at all demanding, and if the Street shifts its tone on the likelihood of Fresenius achieving improved revenue growth and margin targets, the multiple could rerate meaningfully.

I’m just not there yet. I think management is making smart moves, and the shares could offer some value under the right conditions, but my concerns about this remaining a long-term value trap temper my enthusiasm.

data.title: EWN: ASML Proving To Be A Useful Driver, But Caution Warranted Now
data.description:
Introduction

The iShares MSCI Netherlands ETF (EWN), which has amassed total AUM of nearly $360M over a time span of three decades, currently offers coverage to 54 stocks from the Dutch equity market. Over the past year, EWN, whose portfolio is largely oriented around Dutch giant and large caps (~75% of the total portfolio), has done a world of good for its investors, generating solid returns of ~47%, which is around 11% more than a popular diversified European basket, and almost double the returns of global markets.

After this stellar run, how should investors approach EWN now?

Well, here are some of the important themes that could weigh on EWN going forward.

A Lot Hinges on Sentiment Towards ASML

EWN may offer coverage to 54 Dutch stocks, but the clout of each stock isn’t particularly well-spread out with strong concentration effects in play. Do consider that primarily, EWN delivers value for its investors by sampling an index called the MSCI Netherlands IMI 25/50 Index [MNI25I], which is designed to cover 99% of the free-float market-cap of the Netherlands. Even though MNI25I resorts to certain caps (no single stock is permitted to account for over 25% of the index, while all stocks with individual weights of over 5% shouldn’t jointly aggregate to 50% of the index at the time of quarterly rebalancing) to prevent strong concentration effects, such is the buoyancy around the EUV lithography company, ASML Holding NV (ASML), that its stock alone accounts for over 26% of the portfolio (the next quarterly rebalancing will take place in February)!

ASML which has already witnessed a doubling of its share price over the past year is no doubt the single-biggest driver of EWN’s performance, but the question is how long can it keep ascending?

Yes, investors can take heart from the fact that ASML is benefitting from strong AI-related demand with its net bookings in the recently concluded Q4 coming in at an outstanding figure of EUR 13.1B, with booking growth on a YoY basis trending at exceptional levels in H2.

However, investors may also want to consider that ASML’s sales mix this year is likely to be quite sub-par (potentially lower immersion tools sales and higher dry tool sales) and that will prompt a dip in gross margins, something which the company hasn’t been accustomed to for a few years now.

Also, there’s the old bugbear with forward valuations, with ASML now priced at an earnings multiple of around 44x, which works out to a 28% premium over its long-term rolling average.

Paying a premium could be seen as acceptable if ASML was set to deliver sturdy enough earnings growth, but we’ve already noted the dip in gross margins, and further down the line, EPS growth for the year too looks quite underwhelming at less than 14%. Basically, an unappealing forward PEG (Price to earnings growth) ratio of 3.1x may not necessarily drive ample investor sentiment towards ASML.

Dutch Macro Backdrop Looks Mixed

After likely witnessing annual GDP growth of around 1.7% in FY25, the pace of growth in Netherlands is expected to dip to 1.3% in the current year (expected by both major Dutch private institutions - Rabobank as well as ING). Investors should note that this is well below the average annual GDP growth rate of 2% that the economy has been accustomed to seeing since 1996.

It is difficult to imagine growth flourishing when consumers in Netherlands aren’t feeling too emboldened to spend. Note that consumer confidence in Netherlands continues to be in the negative territory for yet another month, with economic expectations by consumer for the next twelve months, deteriorating quite meaningfully in December (from-24 to -32). These damp consumer sentiment readings are quite perplexing to us because spending power is set to be enhanced this year. To elaborate, at the turn of the year, the monthly minimum wage for H1-26 was lifted by 2% YoY, while favorable tax measures are also set to enhance the net pay of Dutch working people this year.

Investors also ought to be mindful of the wobbly trade narrative, as Dutch exports typically account for over 80% of Dutch GDP. Yes, the Netherlands isn’t overly exposed to the US (around 6% of total exports) like some of its other European peers, but Donald Trump recently proposed increasing the tariff rate (which for the Netherlands has been 15%) by another 10% on February 1st on account of the simmering Greenland issue (the tariff rate could potentially increase to 25% by June 1st).

Premium Valuations Don’t Support a Rotation into Dutch Equities Now

The relative strength [RS] charts tell us how EWN’s portfolio of Dutch equities are positioned versus their European peers. You ideally want to buy into this when the current RS looks oversold relative to its long-term mean, coupled with relatively cheaper valuations. Well, that isn’t what we have with EWN now.

Note that the current RS is trading at a slight premium to its long-term average of 0.7x, while EWN’s stocks are priced at an earnings premium of 10% (16.3x) and a cash flow premium of 9% (10.5x) relative to European stocks.

Technicals - Strong Momentum, But Risk-Reward Is Not Ideal

Momentum players are likely latching on to EWN at this juncture as the stock is witnessing a series of higher-highs and higher-lows, and trading above all its key moving averages.

However, we wouldn’t advise new investors to join the bandwagon at this juncture, as the risk-reward on the long-term charts doesn't look very appealing, and likely prompts some caution.

To elaborate, for the last 4 years, EWN’s weekly movements have progressed higher, but they have taken place within the boundaries of an ascending channel (capped by the two black lines). Entering at this juncture isn’t ideal as this week’s candle is taking place outside the channel. Crucially, the candle shape appears to be that of a shooting star candle with a long upper wick reflecting buyer fatigue. For those not familiar with the shooting star, it usually occurs at the end of an uptrend, and points to growing control of the bears on an intermediate basis.

Even if you want to play down the effect of the ascending channel and the shooting star, there is reason to believe that the price action looks stretched as EWN is also trading above its upper Bollinger band (a measure of overextension as it is two standard deviations more than the 20-week moving average).

Investors should also be mindful of EWN’s relatively strong sensitivity to the US markets, which also make it vulnerable to a shift in sentiment there. A beta of over 1x suggests that if the S&P500 witnesses a prolonged downturn, EWN will likely react with even greater fervor to the downside.

Conclusion

While EWN has quite a few things going for it (strong interest in ASML and its pace of bookings, encouraging wage trends in Netherlands, solid momentum triggers), we feel the steep valuation quotient, an unappealing risk-reward backdrop, and potentially slower GDP growth in FY26 dampens the investment case. EWN is a hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla Q4: Fundamentals And Valuation Are Moving Out Of Sync
data.description:
Fundamentals Are Out the Window for This Stock

I previously covered Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) back in July of 2025. I believed it was a Hold then, and even more so now as the stock detaches further from fundamentals. The story then was that CEO Elon Musk was leaving the DOGE department on Capitol Hill and returning to his digs in Austin, Texas. The stock at the time was the most expensive stock in the Mag 7 on just about any fundamental calculation. After running up even more since then, that extreme has widened:

On a forward EV to EBITDA basis, Tesla is now getting close to touching 100X FWD [estimates are anywhere from 70-100X depending on the analyst, 91.54X according to Ycharts]. On the surface, a company like Tesla that largely raises capital through issuing new shares doesn't get dinged if money is raised at more favorable share prices and cash is not wasted on projects that are not accretive to EBITDA.

The multiples expand through dilution if the new capital is spent on CAPEX projects that do not have near-term benefits to EBITDA and do not reduce net debt. This seems to be the mode that Tesla is hunkering into. This is one of the two Mag 7 companies that regularly finances through share sales, the other being Amazon (AMZN). We can see the other 5 of the Mag 7 have share buybacks with falling share counts:

These counts aren't rapidly falling, but they are going in the right direction for shareholders.

My Comments on Earnings

Post Q4 annual report being released, my comments on the earnings were the following:

With another full year of delivery declines, lower GAAP EPS, and revenue contraction, we would assume these to be negatives. Smaller segments, like energy storage, continued to grow at a record 14.2 GWh for FY 2025. FSD and robotaxis are the big story that still keep investors driving this higher, anticipating future, yet-to-be-seen growth. If Musk can keep selling the story, the stock can trend higher regardless of worsening fundamentals. There are few, if any, stocks like it, a pure bet that Tesla will dominate every aspect of physical AI.

How the Headlines Read

Most headlines that I browsed post-earnings release were akin to Tesla beating expectations. Open the articles beyond the headlines, and you see "but," decreasing car sales, decreasing GAAP EPS, and decreasing revenue on a year-over-year basis. If we're being honest with ourselves, analysts and writers have to have a solid basis for calculating growth rates in revenue and earnings and then applying a market multiple to that, similar to how one would draw up comparisons to rental real estate in specific zip codes. If rents or cash flows are not expected to rise or might retract, the price an investor would pay for that property would either stay steady or decrease.

Analysts are still very optimistic about Tesla's earnings potential. We can see high software as a service [SAAS] type growth rates being predicted for EPS through 2029. This expected growth, in my opinion, cannot come from auto sales, energy sales [which are also raw material-intensive lower-margin businesses], or even robot sales [Optimus]. I think it will have to be based on subscription models.

Most believe that Tesla will begin an intensive CAPEX ramp-up for the robo taxi business and possibly further financing of the physical robotics business. This narrative is widely met with positive reception, but the cash generator, the automotive business, is now showing that it has hit a plateau. BYD (BYDDF) has overtaken Tesla as the global EV sales leader. Tesla is the flagship U.S. brand, and several countries, like Canada and the countries of the EU, are signing more deals to import Chinese EVs and opposing U.S. imports.

Digging Into the Report

Segment YoY Change
Energy Generation & Storage Grew +25%
Services & Other Grew +18%
Automotive Shrank -11%

Although the headline reads as if more segments are growing than shrinking, the growing segments are a fraction of the size of the automotive segment.

Segment Percent of Revenue
Automotive 71%
Energy Generation & Storage 15%
Services & Other 14%

With primarily Tesla dealers being able to service Teslas, this has logically funneled a growing revenue number into the system with more vehicles on the road for the services segment. Energy storage, in my opinion, is the only story worth highlighting with the potential for data center energy storage from solar and off-peak hour power plant generation. There is potential to grow a lot here, but the margins will be hard to ascertain. Lithium prices (LC:COM) seem to be heading upward, which could increase the megapack cost of goods sold.

Operating margins are now stabilizing at 5.7%, where they were as low as 2.1% in Q1.

As we can see, both gross and operating margins have been on a downward trend since 2020. Let's compare these margins to a more subscription-oriented company like Microsoft (MSFT):

As we can see, Tesla consistently operates with margins similar to the automotive industry, not the software and subscription industry. The closest hybrid, in my opinion, is Amazon, which runs both subscription/cloud businesses and an asset-heavy retail warehouse business:

In all honesty, this is the best-case scenario I see for Tesla. Being able to hit double the current gross and operating margins. Will that lead to the type of exponential growth analysts expect? That will all come down to sales volume.

Tesla's Current Plan

The main theme of the earnings report thus seems to focus on the robotaxi ramp-up. This is the most near-term realistic driver of earnings and subscriptions to software to create new revenue. From watching Musk speak about the vision, I interpret it as something like this:

  • Robotaxi market expands.
  • Customers get to subscribe to a special FSD service to put their cabs to work.
  • Entrepreneurs buy fleets to put to work.
  • The entrepreneurs grow both automotive sales and software subscription sales.
  • This trend keeps compounding, and Tesla can also operate its own fleets in the most profitable markets.

Risks and Summary

As I mentioned, Tesla is unique because you cannot really put a price on it or put a multiple on it. I think analysts who do intricate calculations in the face of falling numbers in the core business are making it up as they go.

I, personally, believe Tesla is still a Hold. If the auto business perks up and starts to grow again, or if energy grows exponentially faster than expected, I might change my mind if the price doesn't grow out of control in the meantime. On the flip side, as long as Musk is at the helm, holders can still be rewarded as he sells a vision of the future, and people want to invest in that. I don't blame those investors either, but I'm simply an investor who is very rooted in fundamental logic.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Short Selling And Put Buying Point To Big Tech Rally
data.description:
Huge amounts of put buying and short selling in the technology sector point to higher prices in this sector. We wrote about this last December when this activity first began, but it’s even more so today (technology stocks appear ready for another six-month advance).

This conclusion contradicts the forecast of the imminent popping of the tech bubble, which so many writers and analysts have made. Let me show you.

Sentiment is Better at Forecasting Markets Than Valuations

History has shown that valuation ratios are terrible at forecasting the market. Investors appear to be making that mistake again. Prices can stay overvalued for years before correcting.

A far better tool is measuring investor sentiment or expectations. That’s what we specialize in. And right now our tools are saying that there are far too many investors who think this market is going down. The current levels of short selling and put buying are at levels that normally precede a strong rally.

Let's first take a look at short selling.

Short Selling Indicator

This explanation about short selling indicators is taken from last December’s Article:

“From 1940 until 1985, short selling indicators were the primary tool to measure bearish investor expectations. It worked as a contrary opinion indicator. Large amounts of short selling occurred at price lows while little occurred near the top. This was the opposite of what you would want.

Then by 1985, index arbitrage short selling overwhelmed the calculations, making short selling indicators useless.

This changed around 2010 when ProShares created inverse ETF's that both shorted and leveraged the market. These allowed us once again to measure investor expectations using short selling. We found that investor activity in these funds worked in the same contrary way: investors usually bought short funds in high numbers right before prices were about to rise. “

You can see this in this chart. It shows that the purchasing of the ProShare 2X QQQ short fund is the highest in history. It means that record numbers of investors are expecting the technology sector, which is the primary component of the QQQ, to decline.

Corresponding "put buying" in technology stocks confirms this.

Put Buying in the Technology Sector Confirms This

This last chart shows total directional put buying in all 661 technology stocks. Directional put buying in all 661 surged above April levels and hit all-time highs. It shows that on average over 1.4 billion dollars a day are buying puts expecting a price decline in technology stocks.

Conclusion

Our conclusion is the same as the December article.

“These indicators suggest another advancing wave which could be just as powerful as the April to November wave. These indicators also support the idea that the technology sector will again be its leader as it was in the 1st wave.

This is not a short term outlook. We think a second wave will carry into the summer of next year with the technology sector possibly rising another 60% or 70%.

We must reserve for our subscribers "when" we think this second wave will begin.”

data.title: GDXY: Extreme Distributions, And A Tail Risk Not To Forget
data.description:
When I think about a yield-generating solution on gold, I often think of the YieldMax™ Gold Miners Option Income Strategy ETF (GDXY). This is because today, with gold pushed higher by technical and macro drivers and still pronounced implied volatility, covered call solutions could, in my opinion, have room to stabilize in terms of price and distribute distinctive income. So why hold? Well… here too “not all that glitters is gold”: in the sense that although on a theoretical level everything adds up, in practical terms there is a risk that must be confronted. But before analyzing it…

What is GDXY

With an AUM close to $300M, GDXY is an actively managed ETF that was created with the primary objective of generating current income and the secondary objective of maintaining an indirect exposure to the price of GDX (VanEck Gold Miners ETF). It does this through management based on Synthetic covered call and Synthetic covered call spread (alternatively). All with a total expense ratio of 1.08% and a Median 30-Day Spread of 0.06%

Strategy and Composition

The base consists of synthetic long exposure to GDX through long ATM calls and short ATM puts that replicate 100% of GDX movements. At the same time, the sale of calls with strikes typically 0–15% OTM and maturities ≤ 1 month. All using U.S. Treasuries as collateral + source of income.

From the holding distribution file, this exposure clearly emerges.

Result

First of all, there is a reduced MKT vs NAV spread; therefore, it means that pricing is relatively efficient and there are no macroscopic market distortions. Then, this distribution brings the classic consequences of buy-write:

  • Full exposure to GDX drawdowns
  • Mechanically limited upside

  • A distribution that will depend on GDX implied volatility and on the skew structure.

Volatility

In this regard, what is extremely interesting and, for me, decisive in the analysis is the chart (kurtosis + SKEW) shared in the gold outlook for 2026 by the World Gold Council. It communicates two key elements:

  • Increasing kurtosis: return distribution more “fat” in the tails. Typically this means more frequent extreme movements.
  • Structurally elevated SKEW: market willing to pay more for downside protection.

The positive side from a first reading is, in my opinion, one: the market is willing to pay more to hedge, and therefore option premiums (calls and especially puts) increase. This element can therefore support the distribution at the levels defined by the distribution rate (and also explains the reason why today they are so high). On the negative side, let’s not forget the cap on the increase in the price of GDX.

Expectations on GDX

I recently talked about it in this research: I have a positive view on gold for 2026. A conclusion I reach based on 2 drivers:

  1. Expected average demand for 2026 of ~585 tonnes/quarter shared by JPM
  2. Paradoxically favorable macro growth, fueled by declining rates and a weak dollar

Even though I also share a concern: that at the beginning of 2026 the market has already anticipated part of the expected technical growth (the expected demand) in prices. Not by chance, the first target ($5,000/oz) shared by JPM has already been reached, even if it was expected for Q3 2026.

Risk

The analysis of kurtosis and skew highlights greater exposure to extreme risks. In essence, events once considered rare are becoming less exceptional and increasingly more likely.

Considering that GDX and GDXY practically present the same risk profile in bearish markets, it means that potential losses are increasing at an increasingly rapid pace. The concrete risk for GDXY is that the declines of the underlying are so deep as to weigh more than the increase in the premiums collected.

Therefore, in the event of a negative shock, the gap between the premium collected and the added risk (in capital terms) could widen. And it is true that potentially distributions would remain competitive, but the future ability to generate income weakens precisely because of the price collapse and the cap on potential future upside. This would force (in theory and in my honest opinion) to reinvest capital (or the premiums collected) to avoid the deterioration of the distributive capacity of your investment, and it reduces the concrete gain (what an investor actually puts in their pocket), leaving a bit of an “illusion.” And pay attention; this is a risk that anyone should remember when working with buy-write strategies.

My Opinion

I think that the increase in the probability of a tail risk makes an initial entry at these levels quite risky. To minimize the weight of reinvestment and maintain competitive distributions, personally I prefer to average positions in zones below the 40-50 RSI points.

Conclusion

Therefore, I think that the correct rating is HOLD. On GDX, expectations remain broadly positive; potentially part of the growth is already priced in. Kurtosis + SKEW support premiums; therefore, GDXY could continue to maintain positive price stability and a competitive distribution. At the same time, the probability of tail risk increases, which discourages a BUY rating.

data.title: Axcellis Technologies: I'm Not Too Confident In A Recovery
data.description:
Introduction

Axcelis Technologies, Inc. (ACLS) is about to report its Q4 numbers on February 17th, so I thought it would be a great time to give some updates from me regarding what I think about the company’s performance and future potential. It’s been over a year since I gave it a Hold rating and said I was in no rush to add. During that time, I sold my position for a small loss, as I had better use of the capital since then, and I’m glad I did, as the company’s shares underperformed significantly. My stance remains due to deteriorating profitability and no turnaround in sight for now.

Briefly on Performance

Over the past two years or so, the company’s top-line growth has not been there. We can see that as the company hit 2024, it all started to go downhill. The company got hit particularly hard by cyclicality as customers began to digest the inventory post-2023 buildouts. This was particularly evident in customers in China. Throughout 2024, the demand for the company’s products was soft. Booking fell short, and the auto/industrial segments continue to show weakness.

2025 has been more of the same, it looks like, with massive y/y declines in sales due to continual China digestion with mature/power capacity overhang post-2024 buildout. The slowdown in China’s EV growth has been quite a negative development for many companies involved in the space, including ACLS. It seems that there is still no bottom in the near future, but we will have to keep an eye out.

What about the company’s profitability during these trying times? Unfortunately, a bit of the same here, too. As soon as 2024 started with weakening demand, the company’s margins started to decline as well, with a bright spot being that the company’s gross margins remained relatively strong during this downturn, losing around 40% of revenues. The company enjoyed a favorable CS&I mix, high-margin spares, and warranty/install efficiencies, which offset lower volumes. Operating margins have fallen due to the company losing OpEx leverage as it was not able to distribute fixed OpEx as efficiently as before due to lower revenues.

With the noticeable decline in the company’s bottom line, it’s not surprising to see that other key profitability and efficiency metrics followed suit, specifically ACLS’s ROA, ROE, and ROTC. The management has not been able to utilize the company’s assets and shareholder capital as efficiently as before, and it looks like this is going to continue for a while longer, unless we see some good developments in 2026. The company is losing its pricing power and the strong moat it had in such a niche market. At least that is what ROTC tells me.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. As of Q3 ’25, ACLS had around $187m in cash and equivalents, as well as $262m in ST investments and no long-term debt, besides some lease obligations. This position is a saving grace for the company because even if this down cycle continues for much longer, it can easily weather it with its mountain of cash and still profitable operations. Once everything begins to turn around, I expect earnings to rebound massively and share price to begin its ascension, although it already seems to be starting, as its share price is already up 34% in one year, compared to the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust’s (SPY) 15%.

Overall, I think there is very little that could have been done by the management team to improve operations if the demand was just not there. You really cannot force anyone to use your services and products if the customers don’t need them. It is part of the cycle process, and currently, ACLS is experiencing the worst, which may not end just yet.

Comments on the Outlook

Upcoming Earnings

Analysts are expecting the company to make $1.12 and $0.82 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $215m in sales. That translates to around 15% y/y decline, but a slight increase sequentially. Less than 1%, but it’s something. The management’s guidance for the upcoming quarter is more or less in line with the consensus. They are being quite conservative given the ongoing digestion and a pending Veeco merger, which is understandable. There’s no need to be too positive if there is nothing positive to report. Looking back over the last ten quarters, the company consistently beat estimates on top and bottom lines, which indicates to me that the management is being too conservative or analysts are just that much more pessimistic regarding the company’s prospects. Nevertheless, we could see another decent beat on the top and bottom lines coming up.

I’m not expecting any miracles in the upcoming earnings results, but I would like to get some comments regarding what the management sees for 2026. Any comments regarding capacity, demand softness, and the bright spots for the year would be great.

I will be looking out for comments regarding the main revenue contributors, like industrial and auto, but I am expecting quite subdued performance for 2026 still. I used to think that EVs would see a much-needed recovery in 2023 and growth in 2024, only to be proved wrong. Now, we will most likely soon get the EV sales growth for ’25, which should be around 22%, and growth for 2026 is projected to be around 13%, so I want to hear what the management has to say about this development.

What I am really curious to hear about is how the company is going to take advantage of the current HBM, NAND, and DRAM situation, which created a lot of bottlenecks and made many companies a lot more profitable, the main ones being Western Digital (WDC), Sandisk (SNDK), and Micron (MU). I have a position in MU, and I covered all of them in the recent past, giving them strong buys as the demand in the data center space for their products has been absolutely ridiculous. All of them are already out of capacity for the next year, and new capacity is to be built as early as late ’27, so this shortage is going to persist.

ACLS benefits from this surge, too, because ion implanters, which are a critical doping step for wafers, are going to be in a lot more demand. Unfortunately, this business segment is not the biggest in terms of total revenue, so it makes sense why the company hasn’t seen such a massive explosion in revenues. It just doesn’t work like that. However, I want to hear what the management has to say regarding it anyway, because they did say DRAM/HBM is a bright spot for the company. Something that will help its bookings to recover slightly and offset the overall power and general mature digestion.

I want to also hear how the merger is progressing with Veeco (VECO) and what kind of synergies investors expect in the long run. The merger is expected to close sometime in the second half of the year. This move should unlock around $45m run-rate cost savings, and should be non-GAAP EPS accretive in year 1, with cross-selling and tech integration. The merger complements ACLS’s implant doping business with VECO’s anneal, ion beam, and packaging, which, according to the press release, unlocks over $5B TAM in AI/power/packaging segments, creating a factory workflow combo.

Good time to invest in Axcellis?

At the moment, I don’t think so. The company’s operations continue to deteriorate in terms of margins, and I see no end in sight for now. I am also afraid that the demand for HBM, NAND, and DRAM is just not sufficient enough to make me jump in, as I don’t think there is a turnaround just yet. The company is more attractive now than it was before, but I still feel it will be quite the underperformer in 2026, given how other business segments continue their subpar performance. The downturn continues to affect it, but once that turns around, the company will be an attractive buy once again. I am looking forward to hearing what the management has to say about 2026.

data.title: Vistra's Growth Trajectory: Why The Stock Is Poised To Rise, But Better Plays Exist
data.description:
Investment thesis

Vistra Corp’s (VST) recent acquisitions ensure sustained growth over the next few years. With an expected EBITDA of above $7.4 billion by 2027 and debt reduction, my conservative fair value for the stock is estimated at $200 per share.

Background

Vistra Corp is an US integrated retail electricity and power generation company operating in 18 states and serving nearly 5 million residential, commercial, and industrial customers across through several retail brands. Moreover, the company operates one of the largest generation fleets in the country, with about 44 GW of capacity across natural gas, nuclear, coal, solar, and battery storage assets

While most of Vistra’s capacity and production come from gas, in 2024 it acquired Energy Harbor and its four nuclear plants, positioning itself as the second largest nuclear fleet in the US and representing around 20% of Vistra energy production.

Over the last few years most of Vistra’s growth has come from acquisitions, and it appears that this tendency will continue in the future. This month the company announced the acquisition of Cogentrix Energy and its 10 natural gas plants with around 5,500 MW capacity. Although the company is also investing in organic growth, such as with the construction of two natural gas plants with 860 MW capacity.

To be able to fund this growth and maintain visibility, the company hedges and sells under PPAs its energy, as the recent PPA signed with Meta (META) for 2,600 MW. This allows to have clear cash flow visibility until at least 2027 and plan the capital allocation.

Until the year end 2027 the company expects to be able to allocate around $10 billion, distributing about one third of it to shareholders, with the remainder going to growth and debt reduction.

The company plans to base most of shareholder’s returns on buybacks, paying a meager $0.22 quarterly dividend, representing around a 0.5% yield. While I consider Vistra will continue to increase its quarterly dividend, the company is targeting around $300 million per year in dividends, a number that it is already exceeding, suggesting that future increases will be modest.

It seems that the company is using an automatic repurchase program and buying shares regardless of the stock price. I would prefer a more active approach. For example, increasing buybacks now instead of in Q3 when the stock was above $200. Buying back the same amount with the current price would be $30 million cheaper. Alternatively, allocating the buyback budget to dividends instead could increase the yield to around 2%, which is more attractive than the current level.

Financial Position and Valuation

Vistra has a comfortable financial position with $505 million in cash and nearly $19 billion in debt. While debt may seem elevated, it is standard for the sector, and its net debt/EBITDA ratio stands at 2.6x, below the company’s 3x target. Moreover, most of this debt is due after 2030, giving plenty of time to refinance it when needed. In fact, the company recently issued new debt at very attractive rates.

Regarding valuation, the company’s EBITDA guidance for 2026 is between $6.8 and $7.6 billion, representing an increase of nearly 25% over 2025. These numbers are mostly secure as almost all of the 2026 generation production is hedged. For 2027, the company expects a lower growth but still an EBITDA around $7.6 billion.

Assuming that the company achieves the lower end of the 2027 range, which I believe will be increased next year, that uses $2 billion to reduce debt and that maintains a 12x EV/EBITDA, the stock’s price should be $213 per share.

This is why my fair value is around $200 per share, representing more than a 20% upside but difficult to justify repurchases above that level without more positive assumptions.

While I’m long the stock, I prefer AES Corporation (AES), where I expect around 100% upside and strong growth.

Risks

Vistra has several risks. In addition to operational risks, particularly in its nuclear facilities, I consider its main risk to be commodity and electricity price volatility. While most of Vistra’s generation is hedged or sold under PPAs, volatility in natural gas or power prices could still affect margins, especially beyond 2027 when current hedges expire.

Another risk is the credit risk. While current leverage is manageable and the company is reducing its interest expenses. The company needs to refinance its debt from time to time. If credit market is closed due to market turmoil, or persistent higher interest rate, refinancing costs could increase considerably.

Conclusion

Vistra Corp presents consistent growth, and its acquisitions have continued over the last two years, suggesting this trend will persist in the future. Debt remains below the company’s target, which means that the company could continue to acquire companies at attractive valuations to grow.

With the expected EBITDA growth and assuming modest debt reduction, the stock could be valued above $200, representing around 20% upside.
